module: Module 11: Volumes — Cross-Sections, Discs, and Washers

sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Eleven — volumes by cross-sections, discs, and washers. Unit Eight B. Last module the integral measured area between two curves. This module the same integral builds three-dimensional solids. Unit Eight is ten to fifteen percent of the multiple choice, and volumes show up regularly on Section Two.

Slice a loaf of bread. Each slice is thin. Each slice has an area. Add up the areas times the thickness, and you have the volume of the loaf. That is the whole module in one sentence. Every solid we build today is the integral of a cross-sectional area function. Discs and washers are just the case where every slice happens to be a circle.

Three things today. One — solids with known cross-sections, where each slice is a square or a triangle or a semicircle. Two — the disc method, where revolving a region around an axis makes every slice a solid circle. Three — the washer method, where the region does not touch the axis, so every slice has a hole punched out of the middle.

The master formula. Volume equals the integral from a to b of A of x dee x, where A of x is the area of the cross-section at position x. The base region lives flat in the x-y plane; each slice rises out of it, perpendicular to the x-axis. Step one — figure out the side length of one slice from the region: usually top curve minus bottom curve. Step two — plug that side length into the area formula for the shape. Step three — integrate.

Worked example. The base region sits between y equals square root of x and the x-axis, on the interval zero to four. Slice perpendicular to the x-axis. Each slice is a SQUARE. The side length is square root of x — that is the top of the region minus the bottom. Square it: you get x. Integrate x from zero to four. Antiderivative x squared over two. Plug in four: sixteen over two equals eight. The volume is eight cubic units.

Three shape formulas you must memorize. Square — area equals s squared. Equilateral triangle — area equals root three over four, s squared. Semicircle with s as the diameter — area equals pi over eight, s squared, BECAUSE the radius is s over two, a full circle is pi r squared, and a half-circle is half of that. The classic trap — treating s as the radius of the semicircle. It is not. It is the diameter.

Disc method. Take a region that touches the axis of revolution. Spin it around the axis. Every cross-section perpendicular to that axis is now a solid disc — a thin circle. The radius of the disc is the height of the function above the axis. The area of the disc is pi r squared. Stack the discs along the axis with thickness dee x, and the volume is pi times the integral of the radius squared.

Same region as before — under y equals square root of x on zero to four. Now revolve around the x-axis. Each slice is a disc with radius equal to the function value, square root of x. Volume is pi times the integral of radius squared. Radius squared is just x. Integral of x from zero to four is eight. Multiply by pi. Eight pi. The answer is exactly pi times the square cross-section answer BECAUSE the disc area at each x is exactly pi times the square area.

Revolve the same region around y equals negative one instead of the x-axis. Now the disc radius is no longer just the function — it has to stretch from y equals negative one up to y equals square root of x. So the radius is square root of x minus negative one, which simplifies to square root of x plus one. Set up: pi times the integral from zero to four of the quantity square root of x plus one, all squared, dee x. The takeaway you must remember on the exam: revolve around y equals k, shift every radius by k.

Washer method. The region does NOT touch the axis. When you revolve it, there is a hole in the middle of each slice. Each cross-section is a washer — like a metal washer in a hardware store. Outer radius capital R, inner radius little r. Area is pi times the quantity R squared minus r squared. Square first, THEN subtract. The biggest mistake on this topic is writing R minus r, quantity squared. That is wrong.

Worked washer example. The region sits between y equals x and y equals x squared, from zero to one. They meet at zero and one. The line is above the parabola — so when we revolve around the x-axis, the line traces the outer edge and the parabola traces the inner hole. Outer radius x. Inner radius x squared. Square each: x squared and x to the fourth. Subtract: x squared minus x to the fourth. Multiply by pi, integrate from zero to one. One third minus one fifth equals two-fifteenths. Answer: two pi over fifteen.

Now revolve the SAME region around y equals two. Two is ABOVE both curves. Whichever curve sits lower is FARTHER from y equals two — that becomes the outer radius. So the parabola is outer, BECAUSE x squared sits below x on this interval. Outer R is two minus x squared. Inner r is two minus x. Set up: pi times the integral from zero to one of two minus x squared, all squared, minus two minus x, all squared, dee x. The radii flipped roles BECAUSE the axis flipped sides.

Rule of thumb that saves you on every volumes free response. Slice perpendicular to the axis of revolution. Horizontal axis — x-axis or y equals k — slice vertically, integrate dee x, write curves as functions of x. Vertical axis — y-axis or x equals k — slice horizontally, integrate dee y, solve curves for x in terms of y. We will not work a dee y example today, but the disc and washer formulas look identical — just swap every dee x for dee y. Wrong variable, wrong integral, zero setup points.

Your turn. Take the region bounded above by y equals four and below by y equals x squared. Revolve around the x-axis. Step one — disc or washer? Step two — find outer and inner radii. Step three — find the bounds. Step four — set up and evaluate the volume integral. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.

Walking through it. The region sits between the parabola and the line y equals four. They meet where x squared equals four — at x equals negative two and x equals two. The region does NOT touch the x-axis, so it is a WASHER. Outer radius is the line, four. Inner radius is the parabola, x squared. Volume equals pi times the integral from negative two to two of sixteen minus x to the fourth, dee x. Antiderivative sixteen x minus x to the fifth over five. Plug in two: thirty-two minus thirty-two fifths equals one hundred twenty-eight fifths. Double it for the symmetric interval — two hundred fifty-six fifths. Times pi. Two hundred fifty-six pi over five.

The classic shifting trap. Revolve y equals square root of x on zero to four around y equals negative one. Wrong column — student grabs the function value as the radius. Pi times the integral of square root of x, squared, equals eight pi. That is the volume around the x-AXIS, not around y equals negative one. Right column — student shifts. The radius is the DISTANCE from the curve to the axis: square root of x minus negative one, equals square root of x plus one. Square it, integrate. Different number, BECAUSE the axis is a unit lower.

Five checks before you write your final volume answer. One — did you include pi for discs and washers? Two — did you square FIRST and THEN subtract for the washer? Three — did you shift the radii if the axis was y equals k? Four — is your dee x or dee y perpendicular to the axis of revolution? Five — for cross-sections, did you plug the side length into the correct area formula for the shape? Run that checklist. Most points lost on this topic come from those five things.

Recap. Cross-sections: integral of A of x dee x. Plug the side length into the right shape formula. Disc method: pi times the integral of R squared dee x, when the region touches the axis. Washer method: pi times the integral of capital R squared minus little r squared, when the region does not touch the axis. Around y equals k, shift every radius by k. Slice perpendicular to the axis of revolution. Five checks before you write the final answer.

How to actually master this module. OpenStax Calculus Volume Two, chapter six — volumes by slicing, which covers cross-sections plus disc and washer. Khan Academy AP Calculus AB Unit Eight, the volumes lessons. The GIIS Unit Eight B problem set in your dashboard — four volume problems including one with a shifted axis. Book an advisor check-in if the shifted-axis radii still feel fuzzy. Next up — Module Twelve: differential equations and slope fields. The integral now becomes a tool for solving equations.
